We have Ad4s on the button (we're BB, villain called from SB). Wait - seat 0 posted 2 (BB), seat 1 posted 1 (SB) and called. So we're BB with Ad4s. Action is on us preflop, we can check or raise.


With Ad4o out of position against a loose-passive caller, checking makes more sense than raising—we have some showdown value but A4 isn't strong enough to build the pot from out of position, so I'll just check and see what the flop brings.

content.text:
**Analysis:**
- Villain: VPIP 68%, PFR 16% — loose-passive. Folds to flop c-bets 50%, 0 river aggression in 19 hands. Won 7/8 showdowns (plays strong hands to showdown).
- We're in the BB with **Ad4s**, villain called from SB — we're OOP postflop.
- A4o is marginal OOP. A raise could work given villain's passivity, but villain has called from SB and may be trapping (7/8 showdown wins suggests strong holdings at showdown). Checking keeps pot small and lets us c-bet flop profitably given 50% fold-to-cbet rate.

**Decision:** Check and play in position on the flop with a c-bet plan.

content.thinking:
Opponent profile: VPIP 61% (actually meta shows 31 hands out of 51 = 61%), PFR 18% (9/51). Villain calls a lot and rarely folds to c-bets (33%). But folds to river bets 80%.

I have 33 preflop. BB vs BTN/SB situation - opponent called my BB. I'm in BB with option to check or raise.

With a small pair like 33, I can raise to try to take it down preflop, or check and see a flop. Given opponent is loose-passive (calls a lot), raising preflop with 33 isn't great since they'll call and I'll be out of position with a marginal hand. 


Checking makes more sense here—I can set-mine on favorable flops or apply pressure on the river where they fold 80% of the time.

content.text:
**Analysis:**
- Villain: VPIP 61%, PFR 18% — loose-passive, calls a lot preflop
- Folds to c-bets only 33% (2/6) — sticky postflop
- Folds to river bets 80% (4/5) — exploitable late street

**Decision:** I hold `3c 3h` in the BB. Villain just limped/called. Raising OOP with a small pocket pair against a loose-passive caller is -EV — they'll call and I'll be stuck OOP missing my set most of the time. Better to check, see a flop cheaply, and leverage the river-fold tendency if I hit a set or have a good bluff spot.

Opponent is loose-passive (VPIP 54%, PFR 14%). They raised preflop here - with PFR of 14%, this is a relatively strong hand. 

With 22 facing a raise, I have a set-mining situation. I need to call 6 into a pot of 10 (after my call it'd be 16). Stack-to-pot ratio: I have 75 chips, so implied odds need to work.

Call 6, stack left 69. Opponent has 315. Good implied odds for set mining. I'll call.

content.text:
**Analysis:**
- Opponent: Loose-passive (VPIP 54%, PFR 14%). This preflop raise from a 14% PFR villain represents a decent hand (top ~14% of holdings).
- My hand: **22** — a small pocket pair. Best played as a set-mine given the villain's loose-passive tendencies and deep stacks (villain has 315).
- Call 6 into pot of 10 → implied odds: I have 75 behind, villain has 315. Stack/call ratio is 75/6 ≈ 12.5x. Standard set-mine threshold is ~10-15x — this is right on the edge but justified given villain's loose-passive profile (will pay off sets generously).
- 3-betting is risky: villain's PFR is low meaning their raises are stronger; re-raising with 22 as a bluff/semi-bluff isn't ideal.
- **Decision: Call** and look to flop a set.

Board: 9h Ac 3d. I have 8s7s - open-ended straight draw (6 or T completes). Villain bet 10 into 26 pot. I have a gutshot... wait, 8-7 with 9 on board: I need a 6 for 6-7-8-9-T... no. 7-8-9-T-J would need T and J. Actually I have 7-8 and board has 9, so I need T-J? No - 6-7-8-9-T: need a 6 and a T? No, straight is 5 consecutive. 

So I've got an open-ended straight draw with 8 outs - either a 6 or T completes my hand. Villain's playing loose-passive and bet into an ace-high board, so he's probably holding an ace or just floating. The pot odds are 10 to win 36, which means I need about 22% equity to call. With 8 outs I'm sitting at roughly 32% equity on the flop, so calling is the right move.
